nodes.label: Kapampangan Phonology
nodes.summary: Key phonological features: (1) lacks native /h/ — borrowed words retain H, native words do not; (2) D and R are interchangeable (danum / ranum = water); (3) diphthongs /aɪ/ and /aʊ/ reduce to /ɛ/ and /o/ in standard speech; (4) J is pronounced [dy] — ja = [dya]; (5) vowels: A (ə), E (ɛ), I (i), O (o), U (u); (6) predominant CV syllable structure — almost no closed syllables mid-word.

nodes.label: Abakada Syllabary
nodes.summary: The 16 base syllables: AA BA KA DA GA LA MA NA NGA PA RA SA TA WA YA. Each consonant carries an inherent /a/ vowel. Every Kapampangan word is a sequence of V or CV units. This is the foundational framework for verb morphology — identify the first CV syllable of any root, then apply aspect operations mechanically. Taught by native speakers and educators before verb forms are introduced.

nodes.label: VSO Word Order
nodes.summary: Default word order is Verb–Subject–Object, though topic prominence allows flexibility. Examples: Mangan ya ing lalaki. (The man is eating — V–S.) Ikit neng Juan i Maria. (Juan saw Maria — V–Actor–Patient.)

nodes.label: Verb Focus System (Voice)
nodes.summary: Austronesian alignment — the verb changes with affixes to show which noun is the grammatical topic (focus) of the sentence. Five voices: Actor Focus (AF), Object/Patient Focus (OF), Goal/Benefactive Focus (BF), Locative Focus (LF), Circumstantial Focus (CF). This is the single most important feature of Kapampangan grammar.

nodes.label: Actor Focus (AF)
nodes.explanation: The doer of the action is the grammatical topic. Key affixes: mag-, mang-, -um-. Example: Sinulat ku. (I wrote.) — ku = I (ergative actor), sinulat = wrote (completed actor-focus verb). The actor is highlighted as the topic even when other participants are mentioned.

nodes.label: Object / Patient Focus (OF)
nodes.explanation: The thing being acted on is the grammatical topic. Affixes: -en, -an, i-. Example: Silatanan na ku. (He wrote to me.) The recipient (ku = me) is in focus. Used when the object or patient is the most prominent discourse participant.

nodes.label: Goal / Benefactive Focus (BF)
nodes.explanation: The person receiving the benefit is the grammatical topic. Example: Dinan kong pera. (I gave them money.) — the recipient is in focus. Affix: -an on the verb.

nodes.label: Locative Focus (LF)
nodes.explanation: The place or direction of the action is the grammatical topic. Used when location or destination is the most prominent discourse element. Shares -an affix with goal focus — context distinguishes them.

nodes.label: Circumstantial Focus (CF)
nodes.explanation: The instrument, means, or circumstance is the grammatical subject. Affix: i- on the verb. Used for tools, instruments, or the means by which the action is performed.

nodes.label: Progressive Aspect
nodes.formation: Reduplicate (copy) the first CV syllable of the root
nodes.tagalog_contrast: CRITICAL: In Kapampangan, reduplication = ongoing/habitual. In Tagalog, susulat = will write (future). In Kapampangan, susulat = is writing (ongoing). These are REVERSED from Tagalog.
nodes.examples: sulat → susulat (is writing), mangan → mangan (is eating — root unchanged for this verb), basa → babasan (is reading), inum → iinum (is drinking)

nodes.label: Completed Aspect
nodes.formation: Insert -in- after the first consonant of the root. For vowel-initial roots, -in- prefixes the whole word.
nodes.examples: sulat → sinulat (wrote), mangan → mengan (ate — vowel shift), basa → binasa (read), inum → ininum (drank — vowel-initial, -in- prefixed)

nodes.label: Contemplated Aspect
nodes.formation: Insert -um- after the first consonant of the root. For vowel-initial roots, -um- prefixes the whole word. Also uses mag-/mang- prefix forms.
nodes.tagalog_contrast: CRITICAL: In Kapampangan, -um- = future/contemplated. In Tagalog, sumulat = wrote (past). In Kapampangan, sumulat = will write. REVERSED from Tagalog.
nodes.examples: sulat → sumulat (will write), mangan → mamangan (will eat), inum → uminom (will drink — vowel-initial, -um- prefixed)

nodes.label: Noun Case Markers
nodes.summary: Nouns in Kapampangan are marked by case particles. Two noun classes: personal (names of people) and common (everything else). Three cases: Absolutive (topic/subject), Ergative (actor of transitive verb or possessor), Oblique (location or direction). Different particles are used for each class and case combination.

nodes.label: Absolutive Case
nodes.examples: Ing lalaki (the man — topic), Deng lalaki (the men — topic), I Maria (Maria — topic, personal name)

nodes.label: Ergative Case
nodes.meaning: Actor of transitive verb, or possessor
nodes.examples: Ning babai (of the woman / the woman's), Ni Juan (Juan's / by Juan), Dening lalaki (of the men)

nodes.label: Oblique Case
nodes.examples: King bale (in/at the house), Kay Ana (to/at Ana), Karing lalaki (to/at the men)

nodes.label: Pronoun Always-Present Rule
nodes.summary: The pronoun must always be present even when the noun it represents is also stated explicitly. ✅ CORRECT: Dinatang ya i Erning. (Ernie arrived — ya is required alongside i Erning.) ❌ INCORRECT: Dinatang i Erning. (Missing pronoun ya.) This surprises Tagalog speakers but is absolute in Kapampangan.

nodes.label: Enclitic Pronoun Order Rule
nodes.summary: The enclitic (ergative) pronoun always comes first, before another pronoun or discourse marker. ✅ CORRECT: Ikit da ka. (They saw you — da before ka.) ❌ INCORRECT: Ikit ka da. (Order reversed.) This applies universally: enclitic comes immediately after the verb, before any other pronouns.

nodes.label: Absolutive Pronouns (Topic Pronouns)
nodes.summary: Topic pronouns used as the grammatical subject. Usually appear after the verb. Full set: yaku/aku (I, 1sg), ika (you, 2sg), ya (he/she/it, 3sg), ila (they, 3pl), ikami (we exclusive — excludes listener), ikata (you and I, 1pl inclusive 2-person), ikatamu (you, I, and others, 1pl inclusive group), ikayu (you plural, 2pl).

nodes.label: Ergative Pronouns (Enclitic Actor Pronouns)
nodes.summary: Actor-of-transitive or possessor pronouns. Enclitic — they attach after the verb or after the first word in the clause. Full set: ku (I/my, 1sg), mu (you/your, 2sg), na/ne (he/she/his/her, 3sg), mi (we/our, 1pl exclusive), ta/tamu (we/our, 1pl inclusive), yu (you/your, 2pl), da/ra (they/their, 3pl). RULE: always comes before another pronoun or discourse particle.

nodes.label: Portmanteau Pronouns
nodes.summary: Contracted combinations of ergative pronouns. ke = ku + ya (I + him/her): Ikit ke. = Ikit ku ya. (I saw him/her.) kong = ku + lang (I + them): Dinan kong pera. = Dinan ku lang pera. (I gave them money.) These are common contractions in natural speech.

nodes.label: yaku / aku
nodes.meaning: I (topic / grammatical subject, first person singular)
nodes.example: Mangan ya ku. / Yaku i magsulat. (I am the one writing.)

nodes.label: ika
nodes.meaning: you (topic / grammatical subject, second person singular)
nodes.example: Ikaw naman. (You too. / Your turn.) — ika is the full form, often reduced in rapid speech.

nodes.label: ya
nodes.meaning: he / she / it (topic / grammatical subject, third person singular)
nodes.example: Dinatang ya i Erning. (Ernie arrived. — ya is required even with the name present.)

nodes.label: ila
nodes.meaning: they (topic / grammatical subject, third person plural)

nodes.label: ikami
nodes.meaning: we (exclusive — speaker and others, but NOT the listener)

nodes.label: ikata
nodes.meaning: you and I (inclusive — speaker and one listener only, two people total)

nodes.label: ikatamu
nodes.meaning: you, I, and others (inclusive — speaker, listener, and additional people)

nodes.label: ikayu
nodes.meaning: you (topic, second person plural — addressing a group)

nodes.label: ku
nodes.meaning: I / my (actor of transitive verb or possessor, first person singular)
nodes.example: Sinulat ku. (I wrote.) Ing libro ku. (My book.)

nodes.label: mu
nodes.example: Silatanan mu ku. (You wrote to me.)

nodes.label: na / ne
nodes.example: Silatanan na ku. (He/she wrote to me.)

nodes.label: mi
nodes.meaning: we / our (actor/possessor, first person plural exclusive — excludes listener)

nodes.label: ta / tamu
nodes.meaning: we / our (actor/possessor, first person plural inclusive — includes listener)
nodes.example: Mangan ta na! (Let's eat! — ta = we inclusive, includes you the listener.)

nodes.label: da / ra
nodes.example: Ikit da ka. (They saw you. — da comes before ka per enclitic order rule.)

nodes.label: Demonstrative Pronouns
nodes.summary: Kapampangan demonstratives have both singular and plural forms, and distinguish abstract reference (iti/iyan/ita) from concrete reference (ini). Three degrees of distance: near speaker, near listener, far from both. Locative forms indicate place: keni (here, near speaker), keti (here, near listener), karin (there, far). Plural forms: reni/deni, den/ren, reta/deta.

nodes.label: iti
nodes.example: Iting musika (this music — abstract, near speaker)

nodes.label: ini
nodes.meaning: this (concrete physical objects near the speaker)
nodes.example: Ining libro (this book — concrete object, near speaker)

nodes.label: iyan
nodes.meaning: that (near the listener — both abstract and concrete)
nodes.example: Iyang bale mu? (Is that your house? — near listener)

nodes.label: ita
nodes.meaning: that (far from both speaker and listener — both abstract and concrete)
nodes.example: Ita ing bunduk. (That is the mountain — far from both.)

nodes.label: karin
nodes.meaning: there (location far from both speaker and listener)

nodes.label: Ali / E — Verbal Negation
nodes.summary: Negates verbs and statements. ali and e (variant) are largely interchangeable. Ali ya sinali. (He did not buy.) E ke seli. (I did not buy it.) E ku pa mengan. (I haven't eaten yet — pa = yet/still combined with negation.)

nodes.label: Ala — Existential / Possessive Negation
nodes.summary: Negates existence or possession. Opposite of ati (there is / to have). Ala na mung lugud. (There is no more love.) Ala tang nasi. (We have no rice.)

nodes.label: Aliwa — Prohibitive / Contrastive Negation
nodes.summary: Prohibitive form meaning 'Don't' or 'No, not that / not this one'. Used to express prohibition or to contradict. Aliwa ita! (Don't do that! / That's not it!)

nodes.label: Ati — Existence / Possession
nodes.summary: Ati = there is / there are / to have. Opposite of ala. Ati ku pera. (I have money.) Ati la namang konsyensya. (They also have a conscience.) Ati pa? (Is there more? / Do you still have some?)

nodes.label: Ligature (-ng / a)
nodes.summary: The particle -ng (or -g after vowels) links modifiers to the words they modify — similar to Tagalog na/ng. When the modifier ends in a consonant, the linker 'a' is used instead of -ng. Examples: Mayap a bale (a good house — mayap ends in consonant p, use 'a'). Masanting a babai (a beautiful woman — consonant final). Malagung lalaki (a handsome man — malagu ends in vowel u, attach -ng directly → malagung).

nodes.label: Common Discourse Particles
nodes.summary: Kapampangan particles add nuance, stance, and pragmatic meaning to sentences. Key particles: ba (yes/no question marker), na (now/already/yet/anymore), pa (still/yet/more), din/rin (also/too — inclusive), lang (only/just — limiting), daw/raw (hearsay / reportedly), ita (perhaps/probably), naman (also/in turn — contrastive or additive), kung (if — conditional). Particles typically follow the verb or the first word of the clause.

nodes.label: ba
nodes.example: Mengan ka ba? (Have you eaten? — ba marks the question, but questions without ba are also grammatical)

nodes.label: na
nodes.example: Mengan na ka? (Have you eaten already?) / Tara na. (Let's go now.) / Ali ya na. (He/she is no longer here.)

nodes.label: pa
nodes.example: E ku pa mengan. (I haven't eaten yet.) / Ati pa? (Is there more?)

nodes.label: din / rin
nodes.example: Masaya ku rin. (I am also happy.) / Mangan tamu rin. (Let's also eat.)

nodes.label: lang
nodes.example: Isa lang. (Just one.) / Mangan lang. (Just eat.) / Dinak lang. (I just arrived.)

nodes.label: daw / raw
nodes.meaning: Hearsay / reportedly ('they say that…' — distances speaker from claim)
nodes.example: Manyaman daw ing kare-kare. (They say the kare-kare is delicious.) / Meko da raw. (They reportedly went.)

nodes.label: ita (particle)
nodes.example: Maliwanag ita. (It is probably clear.) / Uran ita. (It will probably rain.)

nodes.label: naman
nodes.meaning: Also, in turn, too (contrastive or additive — signals a shift or reciprocity)
nodes.example: Ikaw naman. (You too. / Now it's your turn.) / Masalese ku naman. (I am also fine.)

nodes.label: kung
nodes.example: Kung mangan ta, masaya ta. (If we eat, we will be happy.) / Kung uran, ali taku munta. (If it rains, we won't go.)

nodes.note: Progressive form is identical to the root form for this verb
Contemplated form is identical to the root form
Vowel-initial root — -in- and -um- prefix the whole word rather than inserting after the first consonant
Progressive and contemplated are the same surface form for muli
Completed uses the particle 'na' rather than morphological -in- change
Progressive and contemplated are the same surface form for mako
Completed form is identical to the root form
Progressive and contemplated are the same surface form for ikit
Contemplated form is identical to the root form
Also bili — bili/seli are variant forms of the same verb
Contemplated form is identical to the root form
Contemplated form is identical to the root form
